stimulus: Many trees can be dated by counting their annual growth rings, with each ring representing one year of life. But a researcher claims that this method often can’t be used to identify the age of mangrove trees.
stem: Which detail, if true, would most directly support the researcher’s claim?

answerOptions.content: Mangrove trees grow in warm, salty coastal environments around the world.
Many mangrove trees produce growth rings that are extremely faint or do not appear at all.
Some mangrove forests have been used as fishing grounds by local communities for centuries.
Narrow growth rings can suggest that a tree experienced harsh seasonal conditions.

rationale:
Choice B is correct because if many mangrove trees produce growth rings that are extremely faint or don’t appear at all, then the standard ring-counting method couldn’t reliably be used to identify their age, directly supporting the claim.

Choice A is incorrect: where mangroves grow says nothing about whether their age can be determined by rings. Choice C is incorrect: their use by communities doesn’t address ring detection. Choice D is incorrect: narrow rings could still be counted, so this doesn’t support the claim.
